title: SSL / TLS
objectives: Understand what TLS actually gives you, in plain English
Follow the TLS handshake step by step
Explain why TLS uses both asymmetric and symmetric encryption
Describe the certificate chain of trust

sections.markdown:
**Imagine mailing a sealed lockbox to a friend.**

1. Your friend publishes a **special padlock** — anyone can snap it shut, but only they have the key to open it. That's their **public key**.
2. You put your message in a box, close their padlock on it, and mail it. The mail carrier can carry it — they can't open it.
3. But padlocks are slow, so you actually don't send the whole message that way. You put a **short secret code** in the box first — a shared password you both agree on.
4. Now you and your friend both know the password. Every message after that is scrambled with the password (fast) instead of the padlock (slow).

**That's TLS.** The 'padlock' is asymmetric crypto (RSA or ECDHE) — used once to agree on the password. The password is a symmetric key (AES). All the real traffic is encrypted with the fast symmetric key. Plus, the padlock came with your friend's **certificate** proving they're really them — so a stranger can't trick you into using their padlock.

**TLS (Transport Layer Security)** — the modern replacement for SSL — encrypts data in transit and authenticates the server (and optionally the client). It provides three guarantees at once: **confidentiality** (nobody can read your traffic), **integrity** (nobody can silently change it), and **authentication** (you know you're talking to the real server).

## Why TLS matters, plainly

Without TLS, anyone on the same coffee-shop Wi-Fi can read your traffic, steal your cookies, hijack your session, and inject malicious content into pages you view. In 2015 this was routine — HTTPS was 'for banks.' By 2026, virtually every site uses HTTPS. That is TLS.

As a developer, you rarely write TLS code yourself — libraries handle it. But you *do* need to understand what it's doing, so you can debug expired certificates, pick the right cipher settings, and not disable validation 'to fix an error.'
## The TLS handshake, step by step

sections.code:
Client                                            Server
    │                                                  │
    │ ── 1. ClientHello ─────────────────────────────► │   'Hi. I speak TLS 1.3. Here are the ciphers I support.
    │       (versions, ciphers, random, SNI hostname)  │    Here's a random number. I want to talk to `example.com`.'
    │                                                  │
    │ ◄─── 2. ServerHello ─────────────────────────── │   'I picked TLS 1.3 and this cipher.
    │        (chosen version + cipher, random)         │    Here's my random number.'
    │ ◄─── 3. Certificate + KeyShare ─────────────── │   'Here's my X.509 certificate signed by a CA.
    │        (server's cert chain, ECDHE public part)  │    Here's my ECDHE public value.'
    │                                                  │
    │  4. Verify the certificate chain                 │
    │     climbs up to a trusted root CA               │
    │     (built-in to your OS/browser)                │
    │                                                  │
    │  5. Both sides derive the SAME shared secret     │
    │     from their ECDHE values (never sent!)        │
    │                                                  │
    │ ══════════ 6. From now on, everything is ══════ │
    │            encrypted with AES using that secret. │

sections.markdown: **TLS 1.3 (the version everyone should use) does this in one round-trip.** TLS 1.2 needed two. Older SSL (2.0, 3.0) and TLS 1.0/1.1 are **broken and deprecated** — never enable them.

## Why two kinds of encryption?

A classic trap question. Here's the honest answer.

rows: Authenticate the server + agree on a shared key

sections.markdown: The asymmetric part happens **once per connection**. The symmetric part happens **for every byte after that**. This hybrid is why TLS can protect gigabytes of traffic without melting the CPU.

## The certificate chain of trust

How does your laptop know that a certificate for `google.com` is real? Because it's signed by a **Certificate Authority (CA)** whose public key was **shipped with your operating system** — you trust it before you even boot up.

Here's the chain:

sections.code:
Root CA (built into your OS/browser)
     │  signs
     ▼
  Intermediate CA (issued by root)
     │  signs
     ▼
  Server cert  ('CN = *.example.com', valid 2026–2027)
     │  presented by
     ▼
  Your Flutter app / browser verifies each signature up the chain.
  If ANY signature is wrong, expired, or the root isn't trusted → refuse to connect.

sections.markdown:
## A complete sample program — inspect any site's TLS

This Dart script connects to any HTTPS host and prints the server certificate's subject, issuer, and expiry:
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1 — the worst one: 'trust all certs to fix the SSL error.'**

When you set `onBadCertificate: (_) => true` or use a `TrustManager` that accepts everything, you turned HTTPS into HTTP with fancy overhead. Attackers on the network can now intercept everything. **Fix the actual cert/hostname/clock problem instead.** In production this is a fireable offense.

**Mistake 2: Letting certificates silently expire.**

Every cert has an expiry date. On the day it expires, users get scary browser warnings and your app breaks. Automate renewal (Let's Encrypt + certbot), and monitor with an alert 30 and 7 days before expiry.

**Mistake 3: Assuming HTTPS = safe.**

HTTPS proves you're talking to the *domain you asked for*. It says nothing about whether that domain is trustworthy. `login-google-support-verify.xyz` can have a perfectly valid cert.
## Best practices
**1. Use TLS 1.3 wherever possible.** Fewer round trips, only modern ciphers, no known attacks.
**2. Automate certificate renewal.** Let's Encrypt + ACME is free and battle-tested. Expired-cert outages are one of the top causes of avoidable downtime.
**3. Enable HSTS.** The header tells browsers 'never talk to me over plain HTTP again.' Prevents downgrade attacks.

**4. For high-security mobile apps, consider certificate pinning.** You bake the expected cert (or its public key) into the app. Even a rogue CA can't man-in-the-middle you. Downside: you must update the app when the cert rotates.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Cert inspector.** Use `openssl s_client -connect github.com:443` or Dart's `SecureSocket.connect` to fetch a site's certificate and print its subject and expiry.

**Why:** You'll start noticing which sites are close to expiry — a real security-engineering skill.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Bulk expiry monitor.** Given a list of 10 hostnames, connect to each, get expiry, and print those expiring in <30 days.

**Why:** That's a real ops tool used by DevOps teams. You could ship it as a Slack bot.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Diffie-Hellman by hand.** Implement a toy DH key exchange with small numbers: pick a prime p and generator g, exchange `g^a mod p` and `g^b mod p`, show both sides compute the same `g^(ab) mod p`.

**Why:** Once you've built it, the 'both sides derive the same secret without ever sending it' step of TLS is no longer magic.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Tiny HTTPS server.** Generate a self-signed cert with `openssl`; write a Dart or Node server that accepts TLS connections and responds to HTTP requests. Then have the client verify the hostname explicitly.

**Why:** You'll see both sides of the handshake and understand why hostname verification is a separate step (and often forgotten in mobile SDKs).
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Walk me through the TLS handshake.**

**A:** The client sends a **ClientHello** — the TLS versions and ciphers it supports, a random number, and the hostname it wants (SNI). The server replies with a **ServerHello** picking the version + cipher, its own random, its **X.509 certificate chain**, and its **ECDHE public key share**. The client verifies the certificate chain up to a trusted root CA. Both sides then combine their ECDHE shares to derive the same **shared symmetric key** — this value is never sent over the wire. From that moment on, all traffic is encrypted with AES-GCM (or ChaCha20-Poly1305) using that key. In TLS 1.3 this whole thing takes one round-trip.

**Q2: Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric encryption?**

**A:** Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECDHE) can authenticate the server (because only the real server has the private key) and can be used to safely agree on a shared secret over an insecure channel. But it's very slow — encrypting gigabytes with RSA would burn the CPU. Symmetric encryption (AES) is fast, but requires both sides to already share a secret. TLS combines them: use asymmetric ONCE to authenticate + agree on a secret, then switch to symmetric for the actual data. Best of both worlds.

**Q3: How does the certificate chain of trust work?**

**A:** A server's certificate is signed by an **intermediate CA**. That intermediate is signed by a **root CA**. Root CAs' public keys are pre-installed in your OS or browser — they're trusted before you make any network call. When you connect, the server sends its cert plus the intermediates. Your device checks each signature all the way up to a root it trusts. If any link is missing, invalid, or expired, the connection is refused. This is how a browser can trust a cert for a site it's never seen before.

**Q4: What is a man-in-the-middle attack, and how does TLS prevent it?**

**A:** An attacker sits between the client and server, pretending to be the server to the client and the client to the server. If the client has no way to verify identity, the attacker sees everything. TLS prevents this via the **certificate chain**: the attacker can't forge a certificate for `bank.com` because they don't have the private key that matches, and they can't get a CA to sign a fake one. Any cert they present won't match the chain of trust, and the client refuses to connect. This is exactly why disabling certificate validation is catastrophic — it removes the one thing preventing MITM.

**Q5: What is forward secrecy, and why does ECDHE provide it?**

**A:** Forward secrecy means that even if an attacker later steals the server's long-term private key, they cannot decrypt *past* recorded traffic. Classic RSA key exchange doesn't provide this — the key used to protect data is derived from the RSA private key, so stealing that key later lets you decrypt old sessions. **ECDHE** (Ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman) generates a *fresh* key pair for every connection; those ephemeral keys are discarded when the session ends. Even if the long-term key leaks, past sessions are unrecoverable. TLS 1.3 requires forward secrecy — RSA key exchange is gone.

**Q6: What is certificate pinning, and when should you use it?**

**A:** Certificate pinning is when the client is hard-coded to accept only a specific certificate (or public key) for a given host — not just any cert from a trusted CA. This defends against **compromised or malicious CAs** — someone at a shady CA can't issue a fake cert for your bank and MITM your users. Great for high-security mobile apps (banking, healthcare). Downside: when the real cert rotates, you must ship an app update or clients break. That's why many teams use **key pinning** (pin the public key, which typically outlives the cert) or 'pin multiple' (current + backup) so rotation is less painful.

**Q7: What is HSTS, and why enable it?**

**A:** HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) is a response header — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` — that tells the browser 'never talk to this site over plain HTTP again, always upgrade to HTTPS.' It defends against **downgrade attacks** (an attacker forcing http:// even after you typed https://) and against users accidentally typing the http:// URL. Once set, HSTS is enforced for the specified duration even if the server later returns http links.

## Real-world examples

- **Let's Encrypt** issues free, automated TLS certificates via the ACME protocol. It's the single biggest reason 90%+ of the web is now HTTPS. Certs are valid 90 days; renewal is automatic.
- **Every HTTPS request your Flutter app makes** does this handshake behind the scenes. On a slow phone / cold connection you can literally see the 200 ms of TLS setup before the first byte of your data.
- **When production goes down because 'the cert expired'** — that's a shared moment of shame every DevOps engineer has lived. Auto-renewal + monitoring exists for this exact reason.
- **mTLS (mutual TLS)** — the client also presents a certificate. Used in microservice-to-microservice communication (Istio, Linkerd) so services can be sure they're talking to each other and not an attacker inside the cluster.

## Advanced notes

- **0-RTT resumption in TLS 1.3** — the client can send early data with its very first packet based on a cached session ticket. Great for latency but has a *replay attack* caveat: idempotent requests only.
- **OCSP stapling** — instead of clients checking with the CA whether a cert is revoked, the server periodically fetches an OCSP response and 'staples' it to the handshake. Way better privacy and speed.
- **Certificate Transparency (CT) logs** — every cert issued must be published in a public log; monitors watch for unexpected certs on your domains.
- **Post-quantum crypto** — RSA and ECDHE will be broken by future quantum computers. NIST has standardized lattice-based algorithms (Kyber, Dilithium). TLS 1.3 is being extended to negotiate them.
- **kTLS / offloading** — modern kernels can do symmetric encryption in kernel space or on the NIC, saving copies and CPU on big TLS servers.

flashcards.front: Why both asymmetric and symmetric encryption?
flashcards.back: Asymmetric authenticates + safely agrees on a key (slow). Symmetric encrypts the actual data (fast).
Even if the server's private key is stolen later, past traffic cannot be decrypted. Provided by ECDHE.
Tells the browser to always use HTTPS for this site, defeating downgrade attacks.
It removes the one thing preventing MITM. HTTPS becomes theater.

quiz.explain: After the handshake, fast symmetric encryption protects the data.

quiz.q: Trust in a server certificate comes from…
options: A signature chain up to a trusted root CA
quiz.explain: Root CAs pre-installed in your OS/browser anchor the chain.
Any attacker on the network can now impersonate any server.

options: Even if the server's long-term key leaks later, past traffic stays private
quiz.explain: Ephemeral session keys are discarded and can't be recovered.
TLS 1.2 needed 2 RTTs; 1.3 got it down to 1.

challenges.text: Use `openssl s_client -connect example.com:443` to inspect a real cert; find the expiry date.
In Dart, `SecureSocket.connect` to a site and print `peerCertificate.subject` + `endValidity`.
Given a list of 10 domains, connect to each and print those whose certs expire in <30 days.
Look at any real cert (`openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout`) and identify: subject, issuer, SANs, key algorithm, signature algorithm.
Toy Diffie-Hellman: small p and g, exchange values, show both sides derive the same shared secret.
Generate a self-signed cert; run a tiny HTTPS server (Dart's `HttpServer.bindSecure`); connect from a client that verifies the hostname.
Implement certificate pinning in a Flutter app: reject any cert whose SHA-256 fingerprint doesn't match a hard-coded value. Add a rotation strategy.

interviewQuestions: Walk me through the TLS handshake.
Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric encryption?
How does the certificate chain of trust work?
What is a man-in-the-middle attack, and how does TLS prevent it?
What is forward secrecy, and why does ECDHE provide it?
What is certificate pinning, and when should you use it?
What is HSTS, and why enable it?
Why is disabling certificate validation catastrophic?
